clinicalStem: A 19-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes mellitus is brought to the emergency department by her roommate. She is drowsy, vomiting, and breathing deeply. On examination: BP 88/54 mmHg, HR 122 bpm, RR 28/min, temp 37.1°C. Her breath smells fruity. BGL 28 mmol/L. ABG: pH 7.10, HCO3 9 mEq/L, pCO2 22 mmHg. Urine dipstick: 4+ ketones. Serum K+ 5.8 mEq/L on arrival. What is the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT initial step in management?
options: Start IV insulin infusion immediately at 0.1 units/kg/hr
Administer IV 0.9% NaCl fluid resuscitation
Give IV sodium bicarbonate to correct the acidosis
Replace potassium intravenously before any other intervention
Perform urgent CT head to exclude cerebral oedema
explanation: In DKA, aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with 0.9% normal saline is the most critical first step — it corrects dehydration, improves tissue perfusion, and lowers blood glucose even before insulin is started. Insulin should not precede fluids because it will drive potassium intracellularly, worsening hypokalaemia once K+ begins to fall. Bicarbonate is not recommended unless pH <6.9. Potassium replacement is given once serum K+ falls below 5.5 mEq/L, not at 5.8 mEq/L.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Insulin is a key DKA treatment but must not precede fluid resuscitation; starting insulin first risks precipitous hypokalaemia and cardiovascular collapse.
This is the correct answer — IV 0.9% NaCl is the immediate priority to restore intravascular volume and perfusion.
Sodium bicarbonate is not recommended in DKA unless pH <6.9 and can worsen intracellular acidosis and hypokalaemia.
Potassium replacement is needed in DKA but only once K+ is below 5.5 mEq/L; at 5.8 mEq/L it is not yet indicated and insulin should also be held.
CT head is only indicated if neurological deterioration suggests cerebral oedema; it is not an initial priority over resuscitation.
clinicalPearl: In DKA: fluids first, then insulin (only after K+ confirmed ≥3.5 mEq/L); never start insulin in the setting of hypokalaemia.
keyClue: BP 88/54 mmHg with confirmed DKA — haemodynamic instability demands IV fluid as the first intervention.
commonExamTrap: Candidates jump to starting insulin immediately, ignoring the shock state and the risk of precipitating fatal hypokalaemia.
missedConcept: The sequence in DKA management: fluid → potassium monitoring → insulin; IV fluid is always step one.
redFlagReason: DKA with haemodynamic shock (BP 88/54) is life-threatening; wrong initial management (insulin before fluids in hypotension) can precipitate fatal hypokalaemia and cardiovascular collapse.

clinicalStem: A 74-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and CKD stage 3 is found confused at home. His daughter says he has not eaten well for 3 days and had a chest infection. On examination: dry mucous membranes, skin tenting, GCS 12/15, no Kussmaul breathing. BP 100/60 mmHg, HR 116 bpm. Investigations: BGL 52 mmol/L, Na+ 148 mEq/L, K+ 4.9 mEq/L, urea 18 mmol/L, creatinine 220 µmol/L, serum osmolality 360 mOsm/kg. ABG: pH 7.35, HCO3 22 mEq/L. Urine ketones: trace. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Diabetic ketoacidosis
Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state
Lactic acidosis
Acute-on-chronic kidney disease with uraemic encephalopathy
Septic encephalopathy secondary to pneumonia
explanation: Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS) is characterised by severe hyperglycaemia (>30 mmol/L), markedly elevated serum osmolality (>320 mOsm/kg), absence of significant ketoacidosis (pH >7.3, HCO3 >15), and altered consciousness. This elderly type 2 diabetic fulfils all criteria: BGL 52, osmolality 360, no acidosis, only trace ketones. The precipitant is likely infection. HHS carries a higher mortality than DKA (up to 15–20%).
wrongAnswerExplanations: DKA features significant ketosis, metabolic acidosis (pH <7.3), and elevated anion gap — none of which are present here.
This is the correct answer — the combination of extreme hyperglycaemia, hyperosmolality, absent acidosis, and trace ketones with altered consciousness defines HHS.
Lactic acidosis presents with metabolic acidosis (low pH, low HCO3) and is not associated with extreme hyperglycaemia as the primary finding.
While CKD can cause encephalopathy, uraemic encephalopathy at creatinine 220 µmol/L is unlikely; the extreme hyperglycaemia and hyperosmolality better explain the picture.
Septic encephalopathy is possible but does not account for the extreme hyperglycaemia and hyperosmolality as the primary diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: HHS: glucose >30 mmol/L + osmolality >320 mOsm/kg + no significant acidosis + altered consciousness — mortality up to 20%, higher than DKA.
keyClue: Extreme hyperglycaemia (52 mmol/L), osmolality 360 mOsm/kg, pH 7.35, trace ketones — the triad defines HHS.
commonExamTrap: Confusing HHS with DKA; in HHS there is NO significant acidosis and ketosis is minimal or absent.
missedConcept: HHS occurs predominantly in elderly type 2 diabetics; osmolality drives the altered consciousness, not acidosis.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old man with a 14-year history of type 2 diabetes attends his annual review. He takes metformin and amlodipine. BP today is 148/92 mmHg. Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (ACR) is 65 mg/mmol (previously 32 mg/mmol 12 months ago). eGFR is 61 mL/min/1.73m². Fundoscopy shows background diabetic retinopathy. HbA1c is 72 mmol/mol (8.7%). Which intervention at this visit will have the greatest impact on slowing progression of his renal disease?
options: Intensify glycaemic control by adding a sulfonylurea
Start an ACE inhibitor or ARB
Refer urgently for nephrology outpatient review
Commence a loop diuretic to reduce proteinuria
Add a statin to reduce cardiovascular risk
explanation: ACE inhibitors (or ARBs) are the cornerstone of nephroprotection in diabetic nephropathy with albuminuria, regardless of blood pressure level. They reduce intraglomerular pressure and proteinuria via efferent arteriolar dilation, slowing progression to end-stage renal disease. This patient has macroalbuminuria (ACR >30 mg/mmol) and hypertension — starting an ACEI/ARB is the single most evidence-based intervention for renal protection. SGLT2 inhibitors also have strong evidence but were not offered.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Better glycaemic control is important long-term, but a sulfonylurea does not directly address the immediate priority of reno-protection; moreover it carries hypoglycaemia risk.
This is the correct answer — ACEI/ARB reduces intraglomerular pressure, decreases proteinuria, and slows CKD progression in diabetic nephropathy — the highest-priority intervention.
Nephrology referral is appropriate but not the single most impactful immediate intervention; commencing reno-protective therapy has more direct impact now.
Loop diuretics reduce oedema but do not have a reno-protective effect on diabetic nephropathy and are not indicated here.
A statin is indicated for cardiovascular risk reduction in this patient but does not address renal disease progression specifically.
clinicalPearl: All diabetics with albuminuria (ACR >3 mg/mmol) should be on an ACEI or ARB — this is the most proven intervention to slow diabetic nephropathy regardless of baseline BP.
keyClue: Rising urine ACR (now 65 mg/mmol) with uncontrolled BP in a type 2 diabetic — ACEI/ARB is the reno-protective priority.
commonExamTrap: Focusing on glycaemic control alone and missing the immediate reno-protective benefit of RAAS blockade.
missedConcept: ACEI/ARB reduces proteinuria and slows CKD progression in diabetic nephropathy independently of blood pressure lowering.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes diagnosed 10 years ago attends a routine clinic. She has no visual symptoms. Her last retinal screening 12 months ago showed mild non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR) with a few microaneurysms only. HbA1c is 58 mmol/mol (7.5%). BP 124/80 mmHg. She asks when she should have her next retinal screening. What is the most appropriate follow-up interval for retinal screening in this patient?
options: Every 6 months due to the presence of NPDR
Annually, as mild NPDR with well-controlled diabetes requires yearly review
Every 2 years as her HbA1c is well-controlled
Immediately refer to ophthalmology for laser treatment
No further screening needed unless visual symptoms develop
explanation: Mild non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR) with microaneurysms only and good glycaemic control (HbA1c 58 mmol/mol) warrants annual retinal screening. More frequent review (every 6 months) is reserved for moderate-to-severe NPDR or when HbA1c is poorly controlled. Laser photocoagulation is indicated for pre-proliferative or proliferative retinopathy or clinically significant macular oedema — not for mild NPDR. Omitting screening entirely in a patient with established retinal changes risks silent progression to sight-threatening disease.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Six-monthly screening is appropriate for moderate or severe NPDR, or when risk factors for rapid progression are present; mild NPDR with good control warrants annual review.
This is the correct answer — annual retinal screening is the standard for mild NPDR with well-controlled diabetes.
A 2-year interval is inappropriate once any retinopathy has been detected; annual review is the minimum.
Laser photocoagulation is not indicated for mild NPDR; it is reserved for pre-proliferative/proliferative retinopathy or macular oedema.
Ceasing screening after detection of retinopathy is dangerous; retinopathy can progress silently to proliferative disease.
clinicalPearl: Mild NPDR with good glycaemic control = annual retinal screening; moderate/severe NPDR or poor control = 6-monthly; proliferative or macular oedema = urgent ophthalmology referral.
keyClue: Mild NPDR (microaneurysms only) + HbA1c well-controlled = the trigger for annual — not 6-monthly — follow-up.
commonExamTrap: Over-escalating to 6-monthly screening or laser referral for mild NPDR; the severity of retinopathy drives the interval.
missedConcept: Screening intervals in diabetic retinopathy are risk-stratified by severity of findings and quality of glycaemic/BP control.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old man with a 20-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with a painless ulcer on the plantar surface of his right foot at the base of the first metatarsal. He is a smoker. He reports he did not notice the ulcer forming. On examination: the ulcer is 2 cm × 1.5 cm, non-tender, with a callous rim. Foot pulses are palpable bilaterally. Monofilament testing reveals absent sensation over the plantar surface of both feet. BP 136/84 mmHg, HbA1c 82 mmol/mol (9.7%). Which pathophysiological mechanism is MOST responsible for the development of this foot ulcer?
options: Peripheral arterial disease causing ischaemic ulceration
Loss of protective sensation due to diabetic peripheral neuropathy
Venous insufficiency causing stasis ulceration
Immune dysfunction leading to spontaneous skin breakdown
Autonomic neuropathy causing Charcot joint deformity
explanation: This is a classic neuropathic diabetic foot ulcer: plantar location over a pressure point, painless, non-tender, surrounded by callus, with absent monofilament sensation but preserved foot pulses. Loss of protective sensation from diabetic peripheral neuropathy allows repetitive unnoticed trauma to cause pressure ulceration. Peripheral arterial disease causes painful ischaemic ulcers at tips of toes or over bony prominences with absent pulses — not seen here. Poor glycaemic control (HbA1c 82 mmol/mol) accelerates neuropathy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Peripheral arterial disease causes ischaemic ulcers: painful, at toe tips, with absent pulses — this patient has palpable pulses and a painless plantar ulcer, making ischaemia unlikely as the primary mechanism.
This is the correct answer — absent monofilament sensation indicates lost protective sensation; neuropathy allows unnoticed repetitive pressure trauma to cause plantar ulceration.
Venous stasis ulcers occur over the medial gaiter area (above the medial malleolus) and are associated with varicosities and haemosiderin staining — not plantar location.
While diabetes impairs immunity and wound healing, spontaneous skin breakdown without a mechanical precipitant is not the primary pathophysiology of diabetic foot ulcer.
Charcot joint causes structural deformity of the midfoot but the primary mechanism of this plantar ulcer is loss of protective sensation, not deformity.
clinicalPearl: Neuropathic diabetic foot ulcer: plantar, painless, over pressure points, palpable pulses, surrounded by callus — the hallmark is loss of protective sensation.
keyClue: Painless plantar ulcer + absent monofilament sensation + palpable pulses = neuropathic, not ischaemic, ulcer.
commonExamTrap: Attributing the ulcer to peripheral arterial disease because the patient smokes; palpable pulses and painless presentation clearly indicate neuropathy as the primary mechanism.
missedConcept: Neuropathic and ischaemic diabetic foot ulcers have distinct features; identifying them correctly drives different management priorities.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes has been on metformin 1 g twice daily for 6 years. She presents for review after being recently hospitalised with acute decompensated heart failure and treated with IV furosemide. Discharge eGFR was 28 mL/min/1.73m² (baseline eGFR 55 mL/min/1.73m²). Current medications: metformin, bisoprolol, ramipril, furosemide. She asks if she can restart metformin. What is the most appropriate advice regarding metformin?
options: Resume metformin immediately as the heart failure is now treated
Withhold metformin and reassess renal function in 4–6 weeks
Permanently discontinue metformin given her heart failure diagnosis
Reduce metformin dose to 500 mg twice daily and restart now
Replace metformin with a sulfonylurea as her definitive long-term therapy
explanation: Metformin is contraindicated when eGFR falls below 30 mL/min/1.73m² due to the risk of lactic acidosis accumulation. This patient's acute renal impairment (eGFR 28) requires metformin to be withheld. However, her baseline eGFR was 55 (where metformin is safe at reduced dose) — this is acute-on-chronic kidney disease. The correct approach is to withhold metformin and reassess renal function in 4–6 weeks once the acute precipitant (heart failure/diuresis) resolves. If eGFR recovers above 45, metformin can be resumed at appropriate dose. Permanent discontinuation is premature.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Resuming metformin immediately with eGFR 28 risks lactic acidosis — metformin must be withheld below eGFR 30.
This is the correct answer — withhold and reassess in 4–6 weeks; if eGFR recovers, metformin can be cautiously re-introduced.
Permanent discontinuation is inappropriate — her baseline eGFR of 55 was compatible with metformin use; this is acute-on-chronic impairment from the acute illness.
Reducing the dose does not mitigate the risk below eGFR 30; the drug should be withheld entirely until renal function improves.
Replacing metformin permanently with a sulfonylurea is not warranted — metformin remains the preferred first-line agent if renal function recovers.
clinicalPearl: Metformin should be withheld when eGFR <30 mL/min and during acute illness causing AKI; reassess renal function after recovery before restarting.
keyClue: Discharge eGFR 28 following acute illness — this is below the threshold (30 mL/min) for safe metformin use; withhold and review.
commonExamTrap: Permanently stopping metformin in a patient with acute-on-chronic kidney disease without first reassessing whether renal function will recover.
missedConcept: Metformin safety thresholds: continue at standard dose eGFR ≥60, reduce dose eGFR 45–59, withhold eGFR <30–45 depending on guidelines; always reassess after acute illness.

clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman with known Graves' disease (on carbimazole) presents to the emergency department 3 days after a tonsillectomy. She is extremely agitated, confused, and sweating profusely. On examination: temp 39.8°C, HR 158 bpm (AF on ECG), BP 156/70 mmHg. She has a visible neck swelling and a bruit. GCS is 12. Her carbimazole was withheld pre-operatively and not restarted post-operatively. Which of the following is the most appropriate IMMEDIATE management combination?
options: IV dexamethasone, IV propranolol, oral lugol's iodine, restart carbimazole — in that order
Urgent thyroidectomy to remove the source of thyroid hormone excess
IV hydrocortisone alone and observe for 4 hours
Oral carbimazole alone and restart where it was withheld
Anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation as the primary intervention
explanation: Thyroid storm (thyrotoxic crisis) is a life-threatening emergency precipitated by surgery in an unprepared hyperthyroid patient. Management requires simultaneous: (1) thionamide (carbimazole/PTU) to block new hormone synthesis; (2) iodine (Lugol's) given at least 1 hour AFTER thionamide to block thyroid hormone release (Wolff-Chaikoff effect); (3) beta-blocker (propranolol IV) to control adrenergic symptoms and heart rate; (4) corticosteroids (hydrocortisone or dexamethasone) to block peripheral T4→T3 conversion and treat relative adrenal insufficiency. Carbimazole alone or anticoagulation alone are dangerously inadequate.
wrongAnswerExplanations: This is the correct answer — thyroid storm requires the combination of thionamide, iodine (after thionamide), beta-blocker, and corticosteroid administered concurrently with supportive care.
Urgent thyroidectomy is contraindicated in an uncontrolled thyroid storm — the patient must be medically stabilised first; surgery in this state carries extreme mortality.
IV hydrocortisone alone is insufficient; thyroid storm demands multi-drug therapy targeting synthesis, release, peripheral conversion, and adrenergic symptoms simultaneously.
Restarting carbimazole alone only blocks new hormone synthesis; the vast pool of circulating thyroid hormone will continue to cause the crisis without beta-blockade, iodine, and steroids.
Anticoagulation is a consideration in new-onset AF in hyperthyroidism but is not the primary intervention in a thyroid storm emergency.
clinicalPearl: Thyroid storm requires simultaneous: thionamide → iodine (1 hour later) → beta-blocker → corticosteroid + supportive care. Never give iodine before thionamide.
keyClue: High fever, AF, agitation, confusion in a Graves' patient post-surgery with withheld carbimazole = thyroid storm demanding multi-drug immediate treatment.
commonExamTrap: Giving iodine before the thionamide — iodine given first can transiently increase thyroid hormone synthesis (Jod-Basedow effect); thionamide must always precede iodine.
missedConcept: Thyroid storm is a multi-system emergency; no single drug is adequate — the combination attack on synthesis, release, and peripheral effects is mandatory.
redFlagReason: Thyroid storm is a life-threatening emergency with up to 30% mortality if untreated or mis-managed; immediate multi-drug treatment is essential.

clinicalStem: A 78-year-old woman is brought to the ED in January by her family who found her unresponsive at home. She had stopped collecting her prescription 3 months ago. She is on no current medications. On examination: GCS 8/15, temp 33.5°C, HR 44 bpm, BP 88/54 mmHg, RR 8/min. She has periorbital oedema, a puffy face, dry coarse skin, and a midline neck scar from a previous thyroidectomy. Na+ 124 mmol/L, glucose 2.9 mmol/L, TSH >150 mIU/L, free T4 <2 pmol/L. CXR shows cardiomegaly. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: Oral levothyroxine 50 mcg daily and warm blankets
IV liothyronine (T3) and IV hydrocortisone, with IV glucose and active rewarming
IV thyroxine (T4) alone at high loading dose
Urgent echocardiogram to assess the cardiac cause of hypotension before treating
IV 0.9% NaCl rapid infusion to correct the hyponatraemia and hypotension
explanation: Myxoedema coma is a life-threatening decompensation of hypothyroidism characterised by altered consciousness, hypothermia, bradycardia, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia, and respiratory depression. This post-thyroidectomy patient stopped levothyroxine, precipitating the crisis. IV liothyronine (T3) is preferred over T4 in coma because it is the active hormone and acts immediately without requiring peripheral conversion. Concurrent IV hydrocortisone is essential as adrenal insufficiency commonly coexists. IV glucose corrects hypoglycaemia and active (not aggressive) rewarming is needed. Oral levothyroxine is inappropriate in an unconscious patient and T4 alone delays effect.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral levothyroxine is inappropriate in an unconscious patient (aspiration risk); the dose is also inadequate for an acute crisis requiring IV thyroid hormone.
This is the correct answer — IV T3 for immediate thyroid hormone repletion, IV hydrocortisone for concurrent adrenal insufficiency, IV glucose for hypoglycaemia, and active rewarming.
IV T4 alone is suboptimal in myxoedema coma because it requires conversion to active T3; T3 is preferred for immediate effect, and adrenal support must accompany thyroid treatment.
Delaying treatment for echocardiography in a GCS 8 patient with known myxoedema coma features is dangerous; diagnosis is clinical and treatment should not be deferred.
Rapid IV saline is contraindicated in hyponatraemia — rapid sodium correction risks central pontine myelinolysis; the hyponatraemia here is dilutional and will correct with thyroid hormone treatment.
clinicalPearl: Myxoedema coma: give IV T3 (not oral T4) + IV hydrocortisone immediately — never delay for diagnostics; hyponatraemia corrects with thyroid hormone treatment.
keyClue: GCS 8, temp 33.5°C, HR 44, TSH >150 after stopping thyroid medication in a post-thyroidectomy patient = myxoedema coma.
commonExamTrap: Starting oral levothyroxine in an unconscious patient, or giving IV T4 alone without corticosteroids — both are inadequate responses to myxoedema coma.
missedConcept: Myxoedema coma requires IV T3 + corticosteroids simultaneously — adrenal insufficiency is common and treating thyroid alone without steroids can precipitate adrenal crisis.
redFlagReason: Myxoedema coma carries 30–60% mortality without prompt treatment; GCS 8 with hypothermia and cardiovascular compromise demands immediate IV thyroid hormone and steroid therapy.

clinicalStem: A 42-year-old woman undergoes total thyroidectomy for a 2.2 cm papillary thyroid carcinoma confined to the thyroid gland with no lymph node involvement and no distant metastases (pT2N0M0). She is started on levothyroxine. Post-operatively, her thyroglobulin is undetectable and RAI ablation is deferred pending multidisciplinary review. At her 12-month review, thyroglobulin is <0.1 ng/mL with negative anti-thyroglobulin antibodies. What does this finding most likely indicate regarding her disease status?
options: Likely incomplete structural response — CT neck/thorax should be arranged urgently
Indeterminate response — repeat RAI scan is mandatory within 3 months
Excellent biochemical response — she is likely in remission and long-term monitoring can be relaxed
Biochemical incomplete response — she requires RAI therapy immediately
Structural incomplete response — she requires urgent surgical re-exploration
explanation: In differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC) follow-up, an 'excellent response' is defined as undetectable serum thyroglobulin (<0.2 ng/mL on TSH stimulation or <0.1 ng/mL on suppression) with negative anti-TG antibodies and no structural evidence of disease. Patients achieving this after total thyroidectomy (±RAI) have a recurrence risk of <2% and long-term monitoring can be de-escalated. This pT2N0M0 papillary thyroid cancer with undetectable Tg at 12 months represents an excellent biochemical response consistent with remission.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Structural incomplete response is defined by persistent disease on imaging; with undetectable Tg and negative antibodies at 12 months, structural recurrence is not indicated.
An indeterminate response involves low but detectable Tg levels or equivocal imaging findings; undetectable Tg with negative antibodies does not meet this criterion.
This is the correct answer — undetectable Tg + negative anti-TG antibodies at 12 months = excellent biochemical response, indicating remission; surveillance can be appropriately de-escalated.
Biochemical incomplete response requires persistently elevated or rising Tg without structural disease on imaging — not applicable here with undetectable Tg.
Surgical re-exploration is indicated for structural recurrence confirmed on imaging, not for a patient with undetectable thyroglobulin.
clinicalPearl: Excellent response in DTC = undetectable Tg + negative anti-TG antibodies + no structural disease; recurrence risk <2% and monitoring can be safely relaxed.
keyClue: Tg <0.1 ng/mL with negative anti-TG antibodies at 12 months post total thyroidectomy = excellent biochemical response.
commonExamTrap: Over-investigating or over-treating a patient with undetectable thyroglobulin — recognising the 'excellent response' category allows safe de-escalation.
missedConcept: DTC follow-up uses the ATA response-to-therapy classification (excellent/indeterminate/biochemical incomplete/structural incomplete) to guide surveillance intensity.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old woman with known Addison's disease is brought to the ED by ambulance. She has been vomiting for 2 days following a gastroenteritis illness. She did not increase her steroid dose despite being unwell. On examination: BP 74/40 mmHg, HR 136 bpm, temp 37.9°C. She is confused and difficult to rouse. Serum Na+ 126 mEq/L, K+ 6.1 mEq/L, glucose 2.8 mmol/L, cortisol 44 nmol/L. What is the single most urgent intervention?
options: IM glucagon for the hypoglycaemia
IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat followed by IV 0.9% NaCl and IV glucose
Oral fludrocortisone as she has primary adrenal insufficiency
Request urgent Synacthen stimulation test before starting steroids
IV 3% hypertonic saline to correct the hyponatraemia
explanation: Addisonian crisis (acute adrenal insufficiency) is a life-threatening emergency presenting with haemodynamic collapse, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, and hypoglycaemia in a known Addison's patient with an intercurrent illness and no sick-day steroid increase. Immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg is the cornerstone of treatment — it replaces cortisol, provides mineralocorticoid activity at high doses (negating the immediate need for fludrocortisone IV), and stabilises haemodynamics. This must be given simultaneously with IV 0.9% saline for fluid resuscitation and IV glucose for hypoglycaemia. Never delay treatment to perform a Synacthen test in a known case.
wrongAnswerExplanations: IM glucagon treats hypoglycaemia in the short term but does not address the underlying adrenal crisis, the haemodynamic collapse, or the electrolyte derangements.
This is the correct answer — IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat + IV saline + IV glucose simultaneously forms the emergency treatment of Addisonian crisis.
Oral fludrocortisone is used for maintenance mineralocorticoid replacement but is not appropriate in an acute crisis where IV administration and immediate cortisol replacement are essential.
In a known Addison's patient with typical crisis features, the diagnosis is clinical — performing a Synacthen test before treating will delay life-saving treatment and is inappropriate.
Hypertonic saline is contraindicated in adrenal crisis hyponatraemia; rapid Na correction risks central pontine myelinolysis. Hydrocortisone + normal saline will correct sodium safely.
clinicalPearl: Addisonian crisis: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat is the primary treatment — at high doses it also provides mineralocorticoid cover; never delay for investigations in a known case.
keyClue: Known Addison's + intercurrent illness + missed sick-day rule + BP 74/40 + Na 126 + K 6.1 + glucose 2.8 = classic Addisonian crisis.
commonExamTrap: Delaying hydrocortisone to perform a Synacthen test or spending time treating individual electrolyte abnormalities before giving the definitive treatment.
missedConcept: IV hydrocortisone at stress doses (100 mg) provides both gluco- and mineralocorticoid activity; separate fludrocortisone is not needed in the acute phase.
redFlagReason: Addisonian crisis with BP 74/40 and hypoglycaemia is haemodynamically unstable — without immediate IV hydrocortisone the patient will die; delayed diagnosis is a leading cause of preventable death.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old man with a 3 cm right adrenal mass (confirmed phaeochromocytoma on biochemistry) is scheduled for laparoscopic adrenalectomy in 3 weeks. He is currently on no medications. His blood pressure at the pre-operative clinic is 178/106 mmHg with episodic headaches and sweating. What is the most important pre-operative pharmacological step before proceeding to surgery?
options: Start a beta-blocker (atenolol) as the first agent to control blood pressure
Start alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) first, followed by beta-blockade if needed
Start a calcium channel blocker alone for blood pressure control
Perform surgery urgently without pre-operative pharmacological preparation to prevent crisis
Start combined alpha- and beta-blockade simultaneously on day one
explanation: Pre-operative alpha-blockade is mandatory for phaeochromocytoma surgery — it prevents the severe hypertensive crisis triggered by catecholamine surge during tumour handling. Phenoxybenzamine (non-selective, irreversible) or doxazosin (selective alpha-1) is started 10–14 days before surgery. Beta-blockade (e.g. propranolol) is added ONLY AFTER adequate alpha-blockade is established to control reflex tachycardia. Starting beta-blockade first is dangerous — it blocks vasodilatory beta-2 receptors, leaving alpha-1-mediated vasoconstriction unopposed, causing a severe hypertensive crisis. Adequate hydration and a high-salt diet are also part of preparation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Starting beta-blockers first in phaeochromocytoma is a fatal error — it causes unopposed alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction, precipitating a severe hypertensive crisis.
This is the correct answer — alpha-blockade must be established first (10–14 days pre-operatively), then beta-blockade added if needed for tachycardia; this sequence is mandatory.
Calcium channel blockers alone are inadequate pre-operative preparation for phaeochromocytoma; they do not reliably prevent intra-operative catecholamine surges.
Proceeding without pharmacological preparation is extremely dangerous — unblocked phaeochromocytoma surgery carries a very high risk of intra-operative hypertensive crisis and death.
Simultaneous alpha- and beta-blockade from day one risks the same problem as beta-first if alpha-blockade is not yet fully effective; sequential introduction (alpha first) is the standard.
clinicalPearl: Phaeochromocytoma surgery rule: ALWAYS establish alpha-blockade first (10–14 days) before adding beta-blockade; beta-first causes lethal unopposed vasoconstriction.
keyClue: Phaeochromocytoma scheduled for surgery + uncontrolled BP = alpha-blockade is the mandatory first pre-operative step.
commonExamTrap: Starting a beta-blocker first, which is a classic and dangerous mistake — it causes unopposed alpha stimulation and hypertensive crisis.
missedConcept: The sequencing rule in phaeochromocytoma pre-operative preparation: alpha first, then beta — never the reverse.
redFlagReason: Proceeding to surgery without alpha-blockade, or starting beta-blockers first, risks intra-operative hypertensive crisis, stroke, and death — this is a perioperative safety-critical decision.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman is diagnosed with Cushing's disease (pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma) confirmed by low-dose dexamethasone suppression test failure, elevated 24-hour urinary cortisol, and pituitary MRI showing a 6 mm microadenoma. She has a BMI of 36 kg/m², BP 156/96 mmHg, fasting glucose 7.4 mmol/L, and a vertebral fracture on incidental imaging. She undergoes successful transsphenoidal surgery and cortisol normalises. At her 6-month post-operative review, which complication is MOST likely to require ongoing monitoring and management?
options: Rebound hyperthyroidism due to removal of cortisol suppression of TSH
Relative adrenal insufficiency requiring glucocorticoid replacement
Acute hypercalcaemia from parathyroid hormone activation post-surgery
Diabetes insipidus as the expected permanent complication of transsphenoidal surgery
Acromegaly developing due to secondary GH hypersecretion after cortisol withdrawal
explanation: After successful removal of an ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma, the normal corticotroph cells (suppressed for years by the tumour's excess ACTH and cortisol) require months to recover their function. Patients are therefore at high risk of relative adrenal insufficiency post-operatively. They require glucocorticoid replacement (typically hydrocortisone) until the HPA axis recovers, which is monitored by sequential short Synacthen tests. Failure to replace steroids in this period carries the risk of an Addisonian crisis. Recovery can take 6–18+ months.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Cortisol normally suppresses TSH, but TSH rebound after cortisol normalisation does not cause hyperthyroidism; thyroid function usually normalises uneventfully.

This is the correct answer — post-cure of Cushing's disease, the HPA axis is suppressed and patients need glucocorticoid replacement until the axis recovers; this is the most important ongoing management need.
Hypercalcaemia is not an expected complication of transsphenoidal surgery for Cushing's disease; parathyroid glands are not affected.
Transient diabetes insipidus can occur but usually resolves within days to weeks; it is not the expected long-term complication to monitor for at 6 months.
Acromegaly does not develop after Cushing's cure; GH was also suppressed by hypercortisolaemia and will normalise, not over-secrete.
clinicalPearl: After successful Cushing's disease surgery, ALL patients need glucocorticoid replacement and regular HPA axis testing — the suppressed axis takes months to recover and patients are at risk of adrenal crisis.
keyClue: Successful pituitary surgery for Cushing's disease → suppressed HPA axis → relative adrenal insufficiency is the key post-operative complication requiring management.
commonExamTrap: Assuming that 'cured' Cushing's disease means steroid replacement is unnecessary; the post-operative period carries significant adrenal insufficiency risk.
missedConcept: HPA axis suppression after Cushing's cure is prolonged — glucocorticoid replacement is mandatory post-operatively until Synacthen testing confirms axis recovery.
